sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 106, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: LEFT+RIGHT didn't trigger absorption. The green is still inside the dark outline with px=22. In Level 1, the dark outline had an opening and the green moved through it. In Level 2, the dark outline is closed and the green is already inside.
- Plan: Try moving the green UP out of the dark outline to see if that triggers absorption or changes the dark outline.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 22 of about 109 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After moving UP:
- The green moved from (39-41, 54-59) to (36-38, 54-59) - it moved up by 3 rows
- The W center moved from (40, 56-57) to (37, 56-57)
- The dark outline changed! Before: c px=22 bound=[[38, 53], [38, 60], [42, 60], [42, 53]]
  After: c px=16 bound=[[38, 53], [41, 53], [42, 54], [42, 59], [41, 60], [38, 60], [42, 60], [42, 53]]
The dark outline lost 6 pixels (px=22 -> px=16) and its boundary changed. The boundary now has more corner points, suggesting the shape changed.
This is similar to what happened when I moved LEFT earlier. The dark outline temporarily changes when the green moves out of it.
But the dark outline didn't disappear completely. It still exists with px=16.
Let me think about this differently. In Level 1, the green moved INTO the dark outline and the level was completed. In Level 2, the green is already inside the dark outline, and moving it out changes the dark outline but doesn't absorb it.
Maybe the goal in Level 2 is different. Maybe I need to move the green to a DIFFERENT dark outline, not the one it's currently inside.
Let me look at the dark outlines and greens again:
- Dark outline 1: c px=22 at (8-15, 8-12) - 8x5 rectangle, hollow center 6x3
- Dark outline 2: c px=16 at (38-42, 53-60) - modified shape, was 5x8 rectangle
- Dark outline 3: c px=28 at (41-48, 5-12) - 8x8 rectangle, hollow center 6x6
- Dark outline 4: c px=16 at (50-54, 50-54) - 5x5 rectangle, hollow center 3x3
- Green 1: N px=16 at (36-38, 54-59) - 3x6 rectangle (currently has W center)
- Green 2: N px=16 at (42-47, 33-35) - 6x3 rectangle (has B center)
- Green 3: N px=32 at (45-50, 42-47) - 6x6 rectangle (has B center)
- Green 4: N px=8 at (51-53, 51-53) - 3x3 rectangle (has c center)
The green sizes match the hollow center sizes of the dark outlines:
- Green 1 (3x6) matches Dark outline 2 (3x6 hollow)
- Green 2 (6x3) matches Dark outline 1 (6x3 hollow)
- Green 3 (6x6) matches Dark outline 3 (6x6 hollow)
- Green 4 (3x3) matches Dark outline 4 (3x3 hollow)
Currently, Green 1 is at (36-38, 54-59), which is above Dark outline 2. Green 4 is at (51-53, 51-53), which is inside Dark outline 4.
Maybe the goal is to move each green to its matching dark outline. Green 4 is already inside Dark outline 4. Green 1 needs to go to Dark outline 2, but it's currently above it.
Wait, but Green 1 was inside Dark outline 2 before I moved it UP. Let me move it DOWN to put it back inside Dark outline 2.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to move all greens to their matching dark outlines, and the W center green is the only one that can move in all directions. The B center greens can only move LEFT/RIGHT.
Looking at the positions:
- Green 2 (6x3, B center) is at (42-47, 33-35). Dark outline 1 is at (8-15, 8-12). They're in different quadrants, so Green 2 can't reach Dark outline 1 by moving LEFT/RIGHT.
- Green 3 (6x6, B center) is at (45-50, 42-47). Dark outline 3 is at (41-48, 5-12). They're in different quadrants, so Green 3 can't reach Dark outline 3 by moving LEFT/RIGHT.
This suggests that the B center greens can't reach their matching dark outlines directly. Maybe the W center green needs to swap with the B center greens to give them the ability to move in all directions.
So the process might be:
1. Move the W center green to a B center green's position
2. Click on the B center to swap (the W center moves to the B center green, and the B center moves to the old W center green)
3. Move the now-W-center green to its matching dark outline
4. Repeat for all greens
But wait, in Level 1, the absorption happened when the W center green moved into the dark outline. In Level 2, the W center green is already inside the dark outline, but nothing happens.
Let me try moving the W center green DOWN to put it back inside Dark outline 2, and then see if I can trigger absorption by moving it in a specific way.
Actually, let me first check if the dark outline at (38-42, 53-60) has changed permanently or if it will restore when the green moves back in.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Moving UP changed the dark outline from px=22 to px=16 with a modified boundary. The green moved from (39-41, 54-59) to (36-38, 54-59), exiting the dark outline. The dark outline shape changed when the green moved out.
Plan: Move DOWN to put the green back inside the dark outline and see if it restores. Then try a different approach - maybe I need to move the green to a different dark outline entirely.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move DOWN to put green back inside dark outline
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After DOWN:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W', 'B', 'c') and n['pixels'] <= 32:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
